title: Third Quantization for Order Parameters (II): Local Field Quantization in Superconducting Quantum Circuits
authors: Miao-Miao Yi, Guo-Jian Qiao, Xin Yue, C. P. Sun
abstract: The quantization of superconducting transmission-line resonators is usually introduced phenomenologically by modeling the resonator as an effective LC circuit and imposing canonical commutation relations on macroscopic variables such as charge and flux. Although this approach is highly successful, it leaves open why these macroscopic variables should obey quantum commutation relations and how this behavior emerges from the superconducting state. In this work, starting from the microscopic pairing Hamiltonian underlying BCS superconductivity, we derive the low-energy effective Hamiltonian of a circuit-QED architecture containing a superconducting transmission line with distributed capacitive and inductive elements. We establish quantitative relations between macroscopic observables, including current and voltage, and the spatially local superconducting phase, as well as the microscopic parameters of the electron-phonon system. We then extend the third quantization of the superconducting order parameter, introduced in Paper (I) for the global phase, to the spatially local case. This gives a macroscopic field quantization of the superconducting phase. We show that, after restriction to the low-energy excitation subspace, the local superconducting phase becomes a genuine quantum dynamical variable. Thus, the quantum behavior of transmission-line resonators need not be postulated at the macroscopic level, but follows from the third quantization of the superconducting order parameter. These results suggest that capacitive and inductive superconducting circuit elements share the same microscopic origin, providing a unified framework for superconducting circuit quantization.
summary: This paper provides a microscopic foundation for the quantization of superconducting circuits. Instead of postulating that macroscopic variables like charge and flux obey commutation relations, the authors derive these relations from the third quantization of the superconducting order parameter. They show that the quantum behavior of transmission-line resonators emerges directly from the underlying BCS physics. This establishes a unified framework where both inductive and capacitive elements share the same microscopic origin.
main_problem: Determining whether the canonical commutation relations of macroscopic variables in superconducting circuits are fundamental postulates or emergent properties from the underlying microscopic many-body physics.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the non-commutativity of macroscopic circuit variables (like charge and flux) emerges naturally from the third quantization of the superconducting order parameter, providing a unified microscopic origin for capacitive and inductive elements.
method: The study uses a third quantization framework applied to the superconducting order parameter, deriving a low-energy effective Hamiltonian from the microscopic BCS pairing Hamiltonian using a real-space Gaussian variational approach.
model_or_system: The system consists of superconducting transmission-line resonators and circuit-QED architectures, modeled using the microscopic attractive Hubbard model and the BCS theory of superconductivity in the thermodynamic limit.
key_observables: Local superconducting phase, Cooper-pair-number density, current, voltage, capacitance density, and inductance density.
important_parameters: Microscopic electron-phonon coupling, hopping amplitude (t), interaction strength (U), and the low-energy excitation subspace regime.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the system is restricted to the low-energy excitation subspace, the zero-temperature case, and a spatially slowly varying electron density.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the transition from a global phase to a continuous phase field in the continuum limit; Figure 2 provides a schematic of a 1D superconducting transmission line resonator coupled to a coaxial cable.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the microscopic problem, introduces the third quantization framework for the global phase, extends this to local field quantization, derives effective circuit parameters from microscopic models, and concludes by unifying various circuit elements.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental derivation of the quantum dynamics used in circuit QED, bridging the gap between many-body superconductivity theory and the operational postulates of quantum information hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Starting from a uniform global phase ϕ established in the ground state, fluctuations ϕix emerge at each lattice site, followed by the continuum limit to obtain the continuous phase field ϕ(x).
Figure 2. Simplified schematic of superconducting transmission line

Figure 3. Correspondence between representative superconducting circuit elements, the effective Hilbert spaces in which they are described, the commutation relations emerging in third quantization, and the associated macroscopic observables.
